key_points: Can be applied to scalene, isosceles, equilateral, right-angled, obtuse, and triangles with sides in arithmetic progression.
Used to find inradius r = Area/s and circumradius R = (a×b×c)/(4×Area).
Essential for triangles with decimal sides or where altitudes and angles are unknown.
Can verify triangle properties: right-angle (Pythagoras), obtuse (c² > a² + b²), acute (all angles < 90°).
examples: Triangle sides in AP: 7, 10, 13 → s = 15; Area = √[15×8×5×2] ≈ 34.64 cm²
Isosceles triangle 9, 9, 12 → s = 15; Area ≈ 40.25 cm²; Altitude from base 12 → h ≈ 6.71 cm
Right-angled triangle 7, 24, 25 → Area = 84 cm²; Circumradius R = 7.5 cm
exam_tips: Check if triangle is right-angled or obtuse using side squares.
For triangles in AP, verify triangle inequality before applying Heron's formula.
For decimal sides, carefully multiply (s-a)(s-b)(s-c) to reduce rounding errors.
Use altitude, inradius, and circumradius formulas to answer extended questions.
For equilateral triangles, Heron's formula simplifies to (√3/4) a².
